memoryArtifacts.description:
Mowbray , the name of an Anglo-Norman baronial house, derived from Montbray (Manche) in Normandy south of St Lo. The heraldic badge of the house was a mulberry tree. It was founded at the Conquest by Geoffrey de Montbray, bishop of Coutances. His brother's son Robert de Montbray, who rebelled with him against William Rufus on the Conqueror's death, was made, after their reconciliation, earl of Northumbria, as his uncle's heir but was forfeited and imprisoned for life on rebelling again in 1095. Roger d'Aubigny (of Aubigny in the Cotentin) had two sons, Nigel and William, who were ardent supporters of Henry I. They were rewarded by him with great estates in England. William was made king's butler, and was father of William d'Aubigny (de Albini), first earl of Arundel; Nigel was rewarded with the escheated fief of Robert de Montbray in Normandy and a number of lands in England. Nigel married, by dispensation, the wife of Robert de Montbray, the imprisoned earl, but afterwards divorced her, and by another wife was father of a son Roger, who took the name of Mowbray. Roger, a great lord with a hundred knight's fees, was captured with King Stephen at the battle of Lincoln, joined the rebellion against Henry II (1173), founded abbeys, and went on crusade. His grandson William, a leader in the rising against King John, was one of the 25 barons of the Great Charter, as was his brother Roger, and was captured fighting against Henry III at the rout of Lincoln (1217). William's grandson Roger (1266-1298), who was summoned to parliament by Edward I, was father of John (1286-1322), a warrior and warden of the Scottish March, who, joining in Thomas of Lancaster's revolt, was captured at Boroughbridge and hanged. His wife, a Braose heiress, added Gower in South Wales and the Bramber lordship in Sussex to the great possessions of his house. Their son John de Mowbray, 3rd Baron Mowbray (d. 1361) was father, by Joan of Lancaster, a daughter of Henry Plantagenet, 3rd Earl of Lancaster, of John, Lord Mowbray (c. 1328-1368), whose fortunate alliance with the heiress of John de Segrave, 4th Baron Segrave, by the heiress of Edward I's son Thomas, earl of Norfolk and marshal of England, crowned the fortunes of his race. In addition to a vast accession to their lands, the earldom of Nottingham and the marshalship of England were bestowed on them by Richard II, and the dukedom of Norfolk followed. The 1st duke left two sons, of whom Thomas the elder was only recognized as earl marshal. Beheaded for joining in Scrope's conspiracy against Henry IV (1405), he was succeeded by his brother John, who was restored to the dukedom of Norfolk in 1424. His son John, the third duke, was father of John, 4th and last duke, who was created earl of Warrenne and Surrey in his father's lifetime (1451). At his death (14l8) his vast inheritance devolved on his only child Anne, who was married as an infant to Edward IV's younger son Richard (created duke of Norfolk and earl of Nottingham and Warrenne), but died in 1481. The next heirs of the Mowbrays were then the Howards and the Berkeleys, representing the two daughters of the first duke. Between them were divided the estates of the house, the Mowbray dukedom of Norfolk and earldom of Surrey being also revived for the Howards (1483), and the earldom of Nottingham (1483) and earl marshalship (1485) for the Berkeleys. Both families assumed the baronies of Mowbray and Segrave, but Henry Howard was summoned in his father's lifetime (1640) as Lord Mowbray, which was deemed a recognition of the Howards' right; their co-heirs, from 1777, were the Lords Stourton and the Lords Petre, and in 1878 Lord Stourton was summoned as Lord Mowbray and Segrave. The former dignity is claimed as the premier barony, though De Ros ranks before it... 
Full Description here-
http://bookbinding.com/magna-carta-2/the-barons.html

popularStory.headline: William de Mowbray
popularStory.description:
William was held captive after the Crusades along with Richard I in Germany awaiting ransom.
He was one of 25 Executors of the Magna Carta. 
Found chapel at St Nicholas at Thirsk. Was a benefactor of …

zh.lifeSummary: 當William Mowbray於大約1173年出生在英國越可爾瑟斯克時，他的父親Nigel de Mowbray28歲，母親Mabel de Clare26歲。他與Avice d'Aubigny結婚。他們至少是3個兒子和3個女兒的父母。他於1193年登記服兵役。他去世於1223年3月24日在英國英格蘭林可爾史爾哈可享年51歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭越可爾紐包。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当William Mowbray于大约1173年出生在英国越可爾瑟斯克时，他的父亲Nigel de Mowbray28岁，母亲Mabel de Clare26岁。他与Avice d'Aubigny结婚。他们至少是3个儿子和3个女儿的父母。他于1193年登记服兵役。他去世于1223年3月24日在英国英格兰林可爾史爾哈可享年51岁，埋葬在英国英格兰越可爾紐包。

nameMeanings.name: Mowbray
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (northern; of Norman origin): habitational name from Montbray (Manche). Robert de Mowbray was Earl of Northumbria 1086–95, but was later imprisoned and deposed for rebelling against William II.

variants.name: Mulberry
meanings: English (Middlesex and Cambridgeshire): variant of   Mowbray  .
Irish (Derry): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Maoilbhearaigh ‘descendant of the devotee of (Saint) Bearach’.

variants.name: Mayberry
meanings: English: most probably an altered form of   Mowbray  . It is also found as   Maybury  , which has the form of an English habitational name. There is a place near Woking in Surrey so called; however, this is not recorded until 1885 and is probably derived from the surname. In England this surname is found mainly in the West Midlands; it has also spread into Wales. In Ireland this form is common in Ulster; MacLysaght records that it was taken there from England in the 17th century.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

en.lifeSummary: When William Mowbray was born about 1173, in Thirsk, Yorkshire, England, his father, Nigel de Mowbray, was 28 and his mother, Mabel de Clare, was 26. He married Avice d'Aubigny. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 3 daughters. He registered for military service in 1193. He died on 24 March 1223, in Haxey, Lincolnshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 51, and was buried in Newburgh, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque William Mowbray est né environ 1173, à Thirsk, Everwyck, Angleterre, son père, Nigel de Mowbray, avait 28 ans et sa mère, Mabel de Clare, avait 26 ans. Il a épousé Avice d'Aubigny. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils et 3 filles. Il s'est inscrite au service militaire en 1193. Il est décédé le 24 mars 1223, à Haxey, Lincolnshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 51 ans, et a été enterré à Newburgh, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

de.lifeSummary: Als William Mowbray um 1173 in Thirsk, Yorkshire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Nigel de Mowbray 28 und seine Mutter Mabel de Clare 26. Er hat Avice d'Aubigny geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 3 Söhne und 3 Töchter. Er hat sich 1193 zum Militärdienst angemeldet. Er starb am 24. März 1223 im Alter von 51 Jahren in Haxey, Lincolnshire, England, United Kingdom und wurde in Newburgh, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando William Mowbray è nato il circa 1173 nel Thirsk, Yorkshire, England, il suo padre, Nigel de Mowbray, aveva 28 anni e il suo madre, Mabel de Clare, aveva 26 anni. Lui sposato Avice d'Aubigny. Erano genitori di almeno 3 figli e 3 figlie. Si è arruolato nell'esercito nel 1193. Morì il 24 marzo 1223, nel Haxey, Lincolnshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 51 anni, e fu sepolto a Newburgh, Yorkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito.

ja.lifeSummary: William Mowbrayが約1173年にイギリスヨークシュアイアスイルスクで生まれた時，父Nigel de Mowbrayは28歳，母Mabel de Clareは26歳であった。Avice d'Aubignyと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも3人の息子と3人の娘の親であった。彼は1193年に兵役登録をした。彼は1223年3月24日にイギリスイギリスリンコルンシュアイアハクシーで51歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスヨークシュアイアニューブルグにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: William Mowbray(이)가 대략 1173년 영국 요르크실르 스이르스크에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Nigel de Mowbray(은)는 28세였고, 그의 어머니 Mabel de Clare(은)는 26세였다. 그는 Avice d'Aubigny(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1193년에 군복무를 위해 등록했다. 그는 1223년 3월 24일 영국 잉글랜드 링코실르 하세이에서 51세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 뉴버그h에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando William Mowbray nasceu aproximadamente 1173, em Thirsk, Yorkshire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Nigel de Mowbray, tinha 28 anos e sua mãe, Mabel de Clare, tinha 26 anos. Ele casou-se com Avice d'Aubigny. Eles tiveram pelo menos 3 filhos e 3 filhas. Ele alistou-se no serviço militar em 1193. Ele faleceu em 24 de março de 1223, em Haxey, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom, com 51 anos, e foi sepultado em Newburgh, Yorkshire, Inglaterra, United Kingdom.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда William Mowbray родился около 1173 г. в местности тирск, еркшир, Англия, его отцу по имени Nigel de Mowbray было 28 лет, а матери, которую звали Mabel de Clare, 26. Он женился на женщине по имени Avice d'Aubigny. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 3 дочери. В 1193 году он поступил на военную службу. Он умер 24 марта 1223 г. в местности хакси, линколншир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 51 год и был похоронен в местности нюбург, еркшир, Англия, Великобритания.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando William Mowbray nació aproximadamente 1173, en Thirsk, Yorkshire, Inglaterra, su papá, Nigel de Mowbray, tenía 28 años y su mamá, Mabel de Clare, tenía 26 años. Se casó con Avice d'Aubigny. Fueron padres de por lo menos 3 hijos y 3 hijas. Se enlistó en el ejército en 1193. Murió el 24 de marzo de 1223, en Haxey, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra, Reino Unido, a la edad de 51 años, y fue sepultado en Newburgh, Yorkshire, Inglaterra, Reino Unido.
